step.title: Step 20
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='Move RIGHT to align with neck')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 68, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 67 actions; a strong score needs about 22 or fewer. You are 3.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, so RIGHT worked (moved to cols [29,33]), but UP didn't work twice (blocks stayed at rows [25,26]). This means there's a boundary blocking UP movement at this position.
Looking at the image, the blocks are at rows [25,26] cols [29,33]. The neck is at cols 32-40, but the blocks are at cols 29-33, which is slightly to the left of the neck.
The gray structure boundary shows that at rows 25-26, the structure extends from cols 19-53. The neck starts at row 8 and goes down to row 24, with cols 32-40.
So at rows 25-26, the blocks are in the main body, not in the neck. To enter the neck, I need to move UP to row 24 or above.
But UP didn't work. This suggests there's a boundary at row 25 blocking UP movement. Looking at the gray structure boundary:
boundary=[[8, 32], [8, 40], [16, 40], [16, 39], [17, 38], [24, 38], [25, 39], [25, 53], [49, 53], [49, 19], [40, 19], [39, 18], [39, 14], [25, 14], [25, 33], [24, 34], [17, 34], [16, 33], [16, 32]]
Let me parse this more carefully:
- [8, 32] to [8, 40]: top edge of neck
- [16, 40] to [16, 39]: right edge of neck
- [17, 38]: neck narrows
- [24, 38]: neck bottom-right
- [25, 39]: transition to main body
- [25, 53]: right edge of main body
- [49, 53]: bottom-right corner
- [49, 19]: bottom edge
- [40, 19]: left edge lower
- [39, 18]: left edge indentation
- [39, 14]: left edge further
- [25, 14]: left edge upper
- [25, 33]: transition back to neck
- [24, 34]: neck bottom-left
- [17, 34]: neck widens
- [16, 33]: neck left edge
- [16, 32]: neck top-left
So the neck is at rows 8-24, cols 32-40 (approximately). The main body is at rows 25-49, cols 14-53.
The blocks are at rows [25,26], which is in the main body. To enter the neck, I need to move UP to row 24 or above.
But UP didn't work. This suggests the blocks are blocked from moving UP at this position.
Looking at the boundary, at rows 25-26, the structure extends from cols 14-53. The neck is at cols 32-40, rows 8-24.
The transition from main body to neck is at rows 24-25, cols 33-39 (approximately).
The blocks are at cols [29,33], which overlaps the transition area at col 33. But the blocks are 5 columns wide, so they extend from col 29 to col 33.
To enter the neck, the blocks need to be at cols 32-38 (within the neck width). Currently, they're at cols 29-33, which is slightly left of the neck.
Let me try RIGHT to move the blocks to cols 34-38, which is within the neck, and then UP to enter the neck.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Blocks at rows [25,26] cols [29,33]
# UP blocked - need to align with neck (cols 32-40)
# Move RIGHT to align, then UP

predict(expect_change=True, note='Move RIGHT to align with neck')
action(['RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")

# Try UP now
predict(expect_change=True, note='Move UP into neck')
action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")

# Try UP again
predict(expect_change=True, note='Move UP further')
action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")

# Try UP again
predict(expect_change=True, note='Move UP to top')
action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done', False)}")
print(f"Run complete: {last_action_result.get('run_complete', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
